title: Modify Data and Windchill Modules
description: Modify objects using check out and check in, understand version control and process management, explore access controls, and survey the key Windchill modules.

topics.title: Iteration and Revision Control

content.text: Windchill tracks every modification to an object through a detailed version history. Understanding how versioning works is essential before you modify any product data, because it determines how changes are recorded, who can make changes, and how previous states of an object can be retrieved.
Object Versioning

Every object in Windchill has a version composed of two parts: a revision (letter) and an iteration (number), separated by a decimal point. For example, A.1 is revision A, iteration 1. Out of the box, Windchill uses letters for revisions and numbers for iterations, though administrators may assign unique versioning schemes. Each time you modify and check in an object, the iteration number increments (A.1 to A.2). When you reach a product development milestone and need to create a new revision, the revision letter increments and the iteration resets (A.2 to B.1).

rows: Creates a working copy you can modify; creates a read-only checked-out copy visible to others; locks the object from modification by anyone else
Before making any changes to an object - signals your intent to modify
Removes the lock, stores a new iteration, makes the updated object available to others; previous version kept in history
After completing your modifications - saves changes and releases the lock
Creates a new revision version (increments letter, resets iteration to 1); typically sets life cycle state to In Work
At a product development milestone - before making changes to a released object

content.text: You must check out an object before modifying it. Checking out locks the object so no other user can modify it simultaneously. If you skip the checkout step, your changes cannot be saved. Other users will see a read-only checked-out copy while you work, and the object remains locked until you check it back in.
Version History

Windchill maintains a complete version history for every object. A typical progression looks like this: A.1 (object created) A.2 (modified and checked in) A.2 Released (reached a milestone) B.1 In Work (new revision created for further changes). Business rules may prevent changes beyond certain life cycle states, and the version history enables comparisons between any two versions - whether comparing attributes or graphical representations.

content.prompt: Arrange these version history events in the correct chronological order:
items: A.2 - Object modified and checked in
B.1 In Work - New revision for further changes

keyTakeaways: A version consists of a revision letter and an iteration number separated by a decimal (e.g., A.1, B.3)
Check Out locks an object for your exclusive modification; Check In saves a new iteration and releases the lock
Revise creates a new revision at a milestone, incrementing the letter and resetting the iteration to 1
Version history enables comparisons between any two versions and maintains a complete audit trail

topics.title: Process Control

content.text: Beyond version control, Windchill provides a comprehensive process management framework that governs how objects move through their life cycle. Three core elements work together to ensure that development and review activities follow your organization's established procedures: Life Cycles, Workflows, and Teams.
The Three Process Control Elements

cards.back: Establishes the states an object can exist in - for example, In Work, Under Review, Released, and Obsolete. Life cycles model the commercialization process by defining which states are valid and in what order objects can transition between them.

Routes development and review tasks to the appropriate people automatically. As milestones are completed, workflows move the object to the appropriate life cycle state. Workflows enforce the sequence of approvals and reviews required before an object can advance.

Defines the roles and individuals within a product context. Windchill determines the appropriate person to send tasks to based on their role within the team - for example, sending a review task to the user assigned the Reviewer role.

content.text: How They Work Together

These three elements form an integrated system: the life cycle defines which states an object can occupy, the workflow automates the movement of objects between those states by routing tasks and enforcing approval sequences, and the team determines who receives those tasks based on role assignments. An administrator plans and develops these process elements; end users participate by accepting and completing the tasks assigned to them.

Life cycles, workflows, and teams form an integrated process management system. The life cycle defines what states are possible, the workflow defines how objects move between states, and the team defines who performs the work. Together, they ensure every object follows a controlled, repeatable path from creation to release.

content.prompt: Match each process control element to its role in Windchill:

pairs.right: Defines the valid states an object can exist in and their transitions
Routes tasks automatically and moves objects between life cycle states
Determines who receives tasks based on their assigned role
Plans and develops process elements for life cycles, workflows, and teams

keyTakeaways: Windchill uses Life Cycles, Workflows, and Teams as the three core elements of process management
Life cycles define object states (e.g., In Work, Released, Obsolete); workflows automate transitions between states
Teams determine who receives tasks based on role assignments within a product context
Administrators design the process elements; end users participate by completing assigned tasks

topics.title: Access Controls

content.text: Windchill protects product data through access control - a system of policy rules that determines who can perform which operations on specific objects. Access control ensures that only authorized users can view, modify, or manage product data, and that permissions are automatically adjusted as objects move through their life cycle.
Access Control Rule Attributes

Every access control rule is defined by five attributes that work together to specify exactly what is permitted. For example, a rule might state: "A Reviewer can read the Test Report Documents located in the Drill product if the document is at the Under Review life cycle state."

rows: The area of Windchill where the rule applies
The type of object the rule governs
The maturity level at which the rule takes effect
The user or role the rule applies to
The specific operations that are allowed

content.text: Think of an access control rule as a sentence: "[Actor] can [Permissions] the [Object Type] in [Domain] when it is at [Life Cycle State]." All five attributes must be defined for a rule to take effect. Access can be granted through domains applied to storage contexts, life cycles, or security policies applied to folders and individual objects.
Common Permissions

Windchill provides several permission types that control user interactions with objects. The most common are Read (view the object), Modify (edit attributes and content), Create (add new objects), Delete (remove objects), and Revise (create a new revision). Policy rules combine these permissions with the other attributes to precisely control what each participant can do.

content.prompt: Match each access control attribute to its definition:

pairs.right: The area of Windchill where the access rule applies
The kind of object the rule governs (e.g., Document, Part)
The maturity level at which the rule takes effect
The specific operations allowed (e.g., Read, Modify, Create)

keyTakeaways: Access control rules are defined by five attributes: Domain, Object Type, Life Cycle State, Actor, and Permissions
Each rule specifies exactly what a given actor can do to a specific object type in a particular domain and state
Common permissions include Read, Modify, Create, Delete, and Revise
Access can be granted through domains, life cycles, or security policies applied to folders and individual objects

topics.title: Windchill Modules Overview

content.text: Windchill PLM is a modular platform - organizations select the modules that match their specific business needs rather than deploying a single monolithic system. Each module addresses a different aspect of product life cycle management, from core data management to quality control and supplier management.
Core and Extended Modules

cards.back: The foundational Windchill module. Manages product data including version control, change management, configuration management, CAD design information, product structures (BOMs), and documents. PDMLink is the starting point for most Windchill deployments.

Provides project management capabilities - planning, executing, and monitoring projects. Enables collaborative product development by assigning tasks to team members, tracking milestones, and managing project deliverables alongside product data.

Manages manufacturing process information by integrating product, process, and resource data. Defines manufacturing product structures and processes, and includes the BOM Transformer for converting engineering BOMs into manufacturing BOMs.

Provides simplified access to PLM data for non-PLM users through role-based apps. Offers out-of-the-box applications for specific roles and supports custom app building, letting casual users consume PLM data without learning the full Windchill interface.

content.text: Additional Modules

Beyond the core modules, Windchill offers several additional modules for specialized needs. PartsLink + SUMA (Supplier Management) provides part classification, supplier information, and Approved Manufacturer Lists (AMLs) to ensure parts meet technical and sourcing criteria. Options and Variants enables product variability management through modular design and configurable variant specifications. QMS (Quality Management System) manages product quality including CAPA, nonconformance management, and customer experience management. DTI (Desktop Integration) lets users access Windchill documents directly through Microsoft Office applications - Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Outlook - without opening a browser.

Windchill's modular architecture means your organization only pays for and deploys the capabilities it actually needs. A company focused on design and engineering might start with PDMLink alone, then add MPMLink when ready to integrate manufacturing processes, and QMS when quality management becomes a priority. This incremental approach reduces complexity and cost.

content.prompt: Match each Windchill module to its primary function:

pairs.right: Core product data management - version control, change management, BOMs, and documents
Project planning, execution, monitoring, and collaborative task management
Manufacturing process management - integrating product, process, and resource data
Simplified role-based access to PLM data for non-PLM users

keyTakeaways: Windchill PLM is modular - organizations select only the modules they need for their business
PDMLink is the core module providing product data management, version control, change management, and BOM capabilities
Additional modules like ProjectLink, MPMLink, Navigate, QMS, and Options and Variants extend Windchill for specialized needs
The modular approach allows incremental deployment, reducing complexity and cost

topics.title: Modify a Document

content.text: In this exercise, you will check out a document, upload a modified version, check it back in, and observe how the version changes in the history. This exercise demonstrates the check out / check in workflow and version tracking you learned about in the first topic of this module.

This exercise builds on the saved search you created in an earlier module. You will use the Belt Sander In Work Documents saved search to locate the document. Ensure you have completed the previous search exercises before proceeding.

content.title: Modify a Document
content.objective: Check out a document, upload a modified version, check it back in, and observe the version change in the history.

steps.action: In the search panel of the Navigator, click the Search History and Saved Searches link to access your saved search.
steps.detail: Use your previously saved search to quickly find the document without re-entering search criteria.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as cjohnson/ptc.

steps.action: Click on Belt Sander In Work Documents to perform the advanced search.
steps.detail: The saved search automatically applies your Document type and In Work state criteria, returning matching results.

steps.action: In the search results table, click the View information icon of the Belt Sander User Manual.
steps.detail: Opens the Information page where you can view details and perform actions on the document.

steps.action: On the Information page, click the Actions menu and select Check Out.
steps.detail: Checking out creates a working copy and locks the object from modification by others. This signals your intent to modify the document.
steps.hint: The page will refresh, and a yellow icon will appear next to the object name.

steps.detail: Edit allows you to replace the document content with an updated file while the document is checked out.
This opens a file browser to locate the updated document on your local machine.

steps.action: Browse to W:\WCFD-OVER-Lab-Files\Belt Sander User Manual.pdf and click Open.
steps.detail: You are replacing the document content with the modified version. The original version is preserved in the version history.

steps.action: Click Check In to check the document back in to the system.
steps.detail: Check In removes the lock and stores a new iteration, making the updated document available to other users.

tasks.title: Check In the Document and Observe Version Change

steps.action: In the Check In dialog, type Updated to account for part change.
steps.detail: The check-in comment provides a record of what was changed, helping team members understand the modification history.
The document is checked in and the lock is released.

steps.action: Observe the document name line. The version is shown as A.3.
steps.detail: The iteration number incremented from A.2 to A.3, reflecting the new modification you just checked in.

steps.action: Select the History tab and explore the information on that page.
steps.detail: The change you made is listed at the top of the Version History table, including the check-in comment you entered.

steps.action: Click the Home icon to display your Home page. Note that the Belt Sander User Manual is now listed in the Updates table.
steps.detail: The Updates table on your Home page shows recently modified objects, confirming your change is reflected across Windchill.

keyTakeaways: Always check out a document before modifying it to lock it from other users
Check In saves a new iteration and releases the lock, making the updated document available to others
The version history records every modification, including check-in comments for traceability
Recently modified objects appear in the Updates table on your Home page
